RPD File No. / N° de dossier de la SPR : TB1-13812
TB1-13835 TB1-13855 TB1-13856
TB1-13857 TB1-13858 TB1-13859

Roger Rodrigues
Conseil(s) du (de la/des) demandeur(e)(s) d'asile
Barrister and Solicitor

Représentant(e)(s) désigné(e)(s)

N/A
Conseil du (de la) ministre
[1] XXXX XXXX XXXX (the principal claimant), his common-law partner, XXXX XXXX XXXX, their two sons, XXXX XXXX and XXXX XXXX, their two daughters, XXXX XXXX and XXXX XXXX XXXX, and the principal claimant's mother, XXXX XXXX, all citizens of Hungary, are seeking refugee protection pursuant to ss. 96 and 97(1) of the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act (IRPA).
[2] The principal claimant was appointed by the Board to act as designated representative on behalf of his minor children.
ALLEGATIONS (P.C.)
[3] On November 14, 2010, the principal claimant was returning home with his son XXXX, when they were followed by 8-9 uniformed Magyar Garda members who shouted "you dirty fat gypsy" and threatened to kill them as they tried to run away. They ran into a nearby church however, when the door was closed, the perpetrators caught up with them. Both the principal claimant and his son were beaten and sustained injuries. An elderly couple drove by and indicated that they would call the police. Meanwhile, the principal claimant called a friend to take them to emergency. They also reported the incident to the police.
[4] Due to this incident, the claimants began to save money to leave Hungary. Prior to this in December 2008, their car was set on fire. The fire department sent them a report indicating arson.
[5] In 2006, the principal claimant was going home from a friend's house and observed three suspicious individuals making Roma slurs. A car followed them causing them to have an accident. Both the police and ambulance arrived. The principal claimant was in intensive care for three days and he was forced by the police to indicate that a snow plough had pushed them off the highway.
[6] The principal claimant describes two other incidents in 2005 and 1998. In 2005, the principal claimant, his wife and mother were stopped by security individuals demanding identification, and uttering racial slurs. They went to the local district Roma community office to complain and to the city office, but received no assistance.
[7] In 1998, the claimant was accused of stealing his car, and detained for 72 hours. He was mistreated and, when released, was not given any written reason for his arrest.
[8] The principal claimant is half Roma and half Jewish. His wife is Hungarian.
[9] In his amended Personal Information Form (PIF), the principal claimant indicates that he has been receiving death threats to himself and his family from individuals and organizations on his Facebook page.
ALLEGATIONS OF COMMON-LAW PARTNER
[10] XXXX established a XXXX to assist Roma children between 2001 and 2011. Three or four times yearly, she would receive threats. She reported the telephone calls to the police, but they did not investigate. The claimant alleges that when her employer discovered that her husband was a Roma, she was fired without cause.
ALLEGATIONS OF THE PRINCIPAL CLAIMANT'S MOTHER
[11] The principal claimant's mother describes problems during her school years. She alleges that she was fired from her employment due to being a Roma. She further alleges that she was pushed off a bus in December 2010.
DETERMINATION
[12] The Board finds that the claimants are not Convention refugees or persons in need of protection.
ANALYSIS
IDENTITIES
[13] The identities of the claimants have been established as per the certified true copies of their passports.1
DETERMINATIVE ISSUES
[14] The determinative issues are credibility and state protection.
STATE PROTECTION
[15] The Board, in assessing the issue of state protection, is guided by a number of cases from the Supreme Court of Canada and the Federal Court. The principles, enunciated hereafter, pertain to the case at hand and were accordingly applied. There is a presumption, except in situations where the state is in a complete breakdown, that it is capable of protecting its citizens. A claimant can rebut this presumption by providing clear and convincing evidence of the state's inability to protect. The onus is on the claimant to approach the state for protection in situations where state protection might be reasonably forthcoming.2
[16] No government is expected to guarantee perfect protection to all of its citizens at all times, and the fact that a state is not always successful in protecting its citizens is not enough to justify a claim, especially where a state is in effective control of its territory, has military, police and civil authorities in place and is making serious efforts to protect its citizens.3
[17] Moreover, local failures by authorities to provide protection does not mean that the state on the whole fails to protect its citizens, unless those failures form part of a broader pattern of state inability or refusal to provide protection.4 Indeed, if a claimant believes the actions of some police officers are corrupt, the onus is on the claimant to approach other members of the security forces or the authorities.5
[18] Furthermore, the law states that the burden for the claimant to prove an absence of state protection is directly proportional to the level of democracy of that state.6 The more democratic the state's institutions, the greater the onus on the claimant to show he or she has exhausted all courses of action available.7
[19] While the effectiveness of the protection is a relevant consideration,8 the preponderance of recent Federal Court decisions has held that the test for a finding of state protection is whether the protection is adequate, rather than effective per se.9 A claimant must show that they have taken all reasonable steps in the circumstances to seek protection, taking into account the context of the country of origin, the steps taken, and the claimant's interactions with the authorities.10 In determining whether protection is adequate, it is important to analyze not merely whether a legislative and procedural framework for protection exists, but also whether the state, through the police or other authorities, is able and willing to effectively implement that framework.11
[20] The Board must consider what steps the principal claimant and his family took to seek state protection. The claimants' fears allegedly are as a result of their ethnicity, namely, Roma. However, the principal claimant's wife is Hungarian. The principal claimant testified that he first encountered problems in 1997. When asked why he didn't leave Hungary much earlier, his response was that he had not thought of leaving before the November 2010 incident, following which he had to save the money to leave the country.
[21] The November 14, 2010 incident occurred when the principal claimant and his son were attacked by 4 members of the Hungarian Guard, coming home from soccer practice. Both he and his son required medical intervention.12 A police report was filed.13 The claimants returned to the police three weeks later, but, according to the principal claimant's testimony, he was told that in two weeks he would get a letter saying that the investigation was suspended. The latter information is not contained in the narrative of the Personal Information Form (PIF), which had been amended.14 When confronted with this omission, the principal claimant alleged that he could not get into the police station, which contradicted his earlier statement. Later, the principal claimant testified that he had not returned to the police, "because they did not help me or my son". Moreover, he had not returned because his past experience with the police in 2005, 2006 and 2008 had not been fruitful. These incidents will be discussed below. However, the Board notes from the corroborative evidence15 that the principal claimant was called for a witness interview. The Board finds, given these contradictions, that it is more probable than not, that he did not return to the police station as stated in his testimony and this was an attempt on the part of the principal claimant to embellish the merits of his claim.
[22] The December 2008 incident involved the principal claimant's car being set on fire. The Fire Department filed a report and the principal claimant's wife was summoned as a witness.16 According to the testimony of both claimants, there were a total of 21 such fires, and the perpetrator was apprehended and sentenced to five and a half years in jail. The principal claimant alleged that there would not have been an investigation if there had not been multiple such incidents. The Board concludes that this is mere speculation on the part of the claimants. The evidence before this panel demonstrates that the police did, in fact, apprehend the perpetrator and moreover the principal claimant's wife was summoned to give evidence, thereby also demonstrating that the rule of law was applied.17
[23] In 2006, the principal claimant incurred a car accident, which he alleges was purposely caused by three suspicious characters who were making Roma slurs. They were run off the road and as the principal claimant's driver was attempting to speed away, he hit another "innocent" car. The police and ambulance arrived, and the principal claimant sustained serious injury.18 According to the principal claimant's wife, there was an investigation. However, she reiterated what her husband had said that the police insisted they allege that the accident was caused by a snow plough. She later clarified the purpose of the investigation. She testified that the driver of her husband's car was speeding to escape being hit, and the police were investigating the infraction of driving at excessive speeds. Also an "innocent" car was hit.19 At the first sitting, the principal claimant had alleged that there was a police report, however it was the medical report which was submitted at the subsequent sitting.20 The principal claimant, according to his testimony and corroborated by the medical reports, sustained serious injury, which necessitated a recuperation period of four months. Although the police came to the hospital, given the critical condition of the principal claimant, they were unable to communicate with him. There was no further communication with the police until they came to the claimants' home three months later. The principal claimant's wife was asked why she had not gone to the police in the interim period. Her response was her concern for her husband's health and the care of her children precluded such an intervention. While it is understandable as a young mother and wife that she would be preoccupied, the Board rejects this explanation as it goes to the rebuttal of state protection. The principal claimant alleges that his car hit a Hungarian car. This information is not contained in the PIF and the principal claimant said that that is his opinion today. He further indicated that the only reason for the investigation was that another car was involved. The principal claimant's wife however stated that it was because her husband's driver had exceeded the speed limits to escape.21 Given the foregoing contradictions and omissions, the Board accords less weight to this portion of the claimant's testimony.
[24] There was much discussion surrounding the principal claimant's allegations around threats appearing on his Facebook page.22 When asked why he had Facebook in the first instance, he did not respond. He then indicated that his lawyer had told him not to shut it down. According to his testimony, he acquired it in February 2012, but does not recall when he added security. The Board gives no credence to this portion of the principal claimant's testimony. If the principal claimant is truly fearful and has testified that he not only had a Facebook account, but initially had no security, this flies in the face of his fears. We conclude that this was an attempt on the part of the principal claimant to embellish the merits of his claim.
[25] The principal claimant was questioned with respect to his being identified visibly as a Roma. He acknowledged that he did not resemble a Roma as he is half Jewish. However, he insisted that, during the 2006 incident, the perpetrators knew he was Roma because Roma slurs were indicated. The Board finds that this is mere speculation and is more probable than not implausible, given the fact that the principal claimant was in a vehicle. With respect to the 2008 incident, when the police returned, the principal claimant was unable to provide a reasonable explanation as to how they would know he was a Roma. The principal claimant's wife is a Hungarian, not a Roma. Although she provided a brief narrative, no oral evidence was adduced other than corroborative or additional evidence to support the claim of her husband.
[26] The Board notes for the record the medical reports submitted on behalf of the principal claimant's psychological state.23 However, although post traumatic stress disorder is indicated, there is no indication of a testing to determine the specific level.
[27] The principal claimant's wife corroborated the testimony of her husband. In addition, she provided more detail with respect to the foregoing incidents. She testified that the driver of her husband's car in 2006, following an investigation, was apprehended. However, she was unaware of the penalty. Moreover, she testified that her husband was called in as a witness, information which had not been provided in the PIF or through oral testimony of her husband. However, she testified, in response to counsel, that her husband had told the police the entire story when they came to the house three months later and yet the police told him it was a snow plough that had caused the accident. Moreover later, when questioned by the Member, she acknowledged that she was not present at this interview; she was told to leave the room. Furthermore, since the principal claimant's wife was not at the scene of the accident, she was unaware if there were any witnesses.
[28] With respect to the claimant's children, both the principal claimant and his wife testified on their behalf. The principal claimant does not want them to go through the same hardships and harassment that he and his wife have gone through. He wants them not to be discriminated against if they were to go out to dinner. In short, he wants to be able to protect his children. He went on to describe the actions of the police, in terms of a high percentage of officers who "hit" the Romas. He indicated 7 out of 10, which translated into 70% of the officers being racist. The principal claimant's wife corroborated her husband's fears with respect to the children. She added the fact that her oldest son had difficulties attending school and soccer practice following the incident in which he was injured. She also added that she believes that, if she were to return to Hungary, the attitude towards her daughters would be worse. While the Board accepts that the claimants' son XXXX XXXX would have undoubtedly been traumatized by the 2010 incident, notwithstanding the fact that his father submitted medical evidence, there was no psychological evidence submitted on behalf of their son. Therefore, the Board cannot assess the psychological effects on this minor son. With respect to the principal claimant's allegations that 70% of the police officers are racist, our documentary evidence, while acknowledging police corruption, does not corroborate this assertion.
[29] The principal claimant's mother testified that she concurred with the testimony provided by her son and his wife. She indicated that the police do not help; "when we call them for any reason, they would make notes but this is where it stops". She provided no further detail and did not testify regarding the information contained in the narrative of her Personal Information Form. Therefore, given the scant information, the Board concludes that she has not rebutted the presumption of state protection.
[30] There was documentary evidence submitted by the principal claimant's brother,24 which indicates graffiti placed on their home. However, the Board notes that a very thorough investigation was conducted in this regard, thereby demonstrating that the rule of law prevailed.
CONCLUSION
[31] With respect to the principal claimant's mother, there was no evidence adduced that would persuade the Board that she would be unable to access state protection if she were to return to Hungary today. The onus is on her to provide "clear and convincing" evidence of the state's inability to protect her.
[32] With respect to the claimants' children, while we may sympathize with the claimants' desire to provide a better life for their children, their fears are, for the most part, in the future and speculative. We do acknowledge that their son XXXX XXXX suffered a trauma in 2010. However, this one isolated incident, particularly in the absence of psychological corroborative evidence, does not in the Board's opinion constitute persecution. Accordingly, the Board finds that these minor claimants have not rebutted the presumption of state protection with "clear and convincing" evidence of the state's inability to protect them.
[33] With respect to the principal claimant's wife, as noted above, she is Hungarian and therefore her fears are not reflected as a Roma. Nevertheless, she is the wife of the principal claimant and therefore his fears carry over to her.
[34] With respect to the principal claimant we note the following:-
2006 CAR ACCIDENT
[35] We acknowledge that this incident was serious, in that it caused critical injury to the principal claimant. As per Maldonado,25 we accept the principal claimant's testimony that he was told that a snow plough was the cause, yet he knows otherwise. However, we do have problems with the fact that this was caused due to ethnicity. We find it implausible that the perpetrators would call out racial slurs when looking at the principal claimant driving in a car. An investigation took place, although from the testimony it appears that this related to the speed and perhaps the fact that an "innocent" car was involved.
2008 FIRE
[36] The law prevailed and the perpetrator was appropriately apprehended with a lengthy jail sentence.
2010 ASSAULT
[37] Given the corroborative evidence,26 the Board finds that the authorities acted appropriately. A report was taken, followed by a witness interview of the principal claimant.
2012 INCIDENT (according to his brother)
[38] Given the corroborative evidence,27 the Board finds that the authorities acted appropriately according to the reports submitted.
FACEBOOK
[39] I have already determined that this portion of the testimony and corroborative evidence28 is not accorded much weight.
CONCLUSION
[40] Firstly, we note the credibility concerns as enunciated above. While the claimants have sustained some unfortunate incidents, and we do accept that the 2006 incident was serious, in the Board's opinion, they have not provided "clear and convincing" evidence of the state's inability to protect them. Moreover, we find that their fears based on their ethnicity, namely, Roma, are not substantiated. In totality, they have not rebutted the presumption of state protection.
[41] Documentary evidence indicates that Hungary is a republic with a population of approximately 10 million and a multiparty parliamentary democracy. Legislative authority is vested in the unicameral parliament (National Assembly). The elections held in 2006 were assessed as free and fair. Civilian authorities generally maintained effective control of the security forces.
[42] The constitution and law prohibit arbitrary arrest and detention, and the government generally observed these prohibitions. According to the Prosecutor General's Office, there were 65 official complaints of arbitrary detention. The Prosecutor General's office rejected 22 complaints and initiated indictments in three cases.
[43] The Hungarian National Police (HNP), which operates under the direction of the Ministry of Justice and Law Enforcement, is responsible for enforcing laws and maintaining order nationwide. Twenty regional police departments are directly subordinate to the HNP; city police are subordinate to the regional police and have local jurisdiction: the Republican Guard; the National Bureau of Investigation; and the Emergency Police, which are also responsible for counterterrorism and crowd control.
[44] Corruption within police agencies remained a problem. Penalties for police officers found guilty of wrongdoing include reprimand, dismissal, and criminal prosecution. The Independent Police Complaints Board (IPCB), established in 2008, continued to investigate violations and omissions by the police that substantively concerned fundamental rights. The five-member body, appointed by a two-thirds majority of the National Assembly, functions independently of police authorities.29
[45] I would be remiss if I did not acknowledge and consider that there is information in the documentation to indicate that there is widespread reporting of incidents of intolerance, discrimination and persecution of Romani individuals in Hungary. However, weighted against this is persuasive evidence that indicates that Hungary candidly acknowledges its past problems and is making serious efforts to rectify the treatment of minorities in that country, especially in the case of the Roma. The Board recognizes that there are some inconsistencies among several sources within the documentary evidence; however, the preponderance of the objective evidence regarding current country conditions suggests that, although not perfect, there is an adequate state protection in Hungary for Roma who are victims of crime, police abuse, discrimination or persecution, that Hungary is making serious efforts to address these problems, and that the police and government officials are both willing and able to protect victims.
[46] There is inconsiderable activism in Hungary by the country's ethnic minorities for the protection of their rights. In July 1993, the government adopted legislation guaranteeing the cultural, civil and political rights of 12 minority groups and prohibiting ethnic discrimination. Following the approval of this legislation, minority rights activists launched a new campaign to change Hungary's electoral law, with the aim of securing the direct representation of ethnic groups in the legislature. In the 1994 municipal elections, ethnic minorities were able to elect their own local ethnic authorities, with consultative roles on cultural and educational issues affecting the community. In February 1995 Hungary signed the counsel of Europe Convention on the Protection of National Minorities. In April, Hungary elected their own governing body, the National Autonomous Authority of the Romany minority, which was in power to administer funds and deliberate issues affecting the Roma. In December 2008 the Metropolitan Court in Budapest ordered the dissolution of the extreme nationalist Hungarian Guard, which was responsible for attacks and discriminatory behaviour against the Roma community. A court of appeal upheld the ruling, stating that the organization incited violence and violated the freedom of others. This prohibition was confirmed by the Supreme Court in December, 2008.30
[47] Roma, like the other 12 official minorities, are entitled to elect their own minority self-government's (MSGs), which organized minority activities and handle cultural and educational affairs. The president of each MSG also has the right to attend and speak at local government assemblies.31
[48] While there are reports of police corruption, and that police sometimes use excessive force against Roma, the evidence32 also shows that the state takes action when complaints are made. Sources indicate that the Independent Police Complaints Board (IPCB) began operation in January 2008. The IPCB, which is independent of the police, reviews complaints of police actions which violate fundamental rights and makes recommendations to the head of the National Police. If the recommendations are not accepted, the matter can be referred to a court. The Budapest-based NGO European Roma Rights Ce Center (ERRC) describes the IPCB as a credible and independent watchdog to ensure the accountability of the police and has called for the government to ensure the IPCB's independence and strengthen its mandate. However, it is to be noted that there is criticism that the police followed up on only a small proportion of the IPCB's recommendations.
[49] The Parliamentary Commissioner for National and Ethnic Minority Rights (Minority Ombudsman) takes complaints from any person that feels that as a result of a government agency's decision, proceedings or negligence, a violation has taken place in her/his national or ethnic minority rights, or that the direct threat of such a violation is imminent. The documentary evidence indicates many other similar initiatives undertaken by the Hungarian government to address the problems of corruption within its police forces and to provide measures or avenues of complaint to the public.33
[50] According to the documents, police officers found guilty of misconduct in Hungary may face reprimand, dismissal and/or criminal prosecution. The documents indicate that 2458 police officers were found guilty of "breaching discipline," 716 were found guilty of misdemeanor offenses, 26 were found unfit for duty, 4 received prison sentences, 28 received suspended sentences, 213 were fined, 5 were demoted, 26 were dismissed, 42 were reprimanded, and 10 were convicted of corruption.
[51] The evidence indicates that police do still commit abuses against people, including the Roma, but there is also evidence that indicates it is reasonable to expect authorities to take action in these cases.
[52] Relating to the Magyar Garda (Hungarian Guard), on December 15, 2009 the Supreme Court upheld an earlier appeals court decision dissolving the far-right paramilitary group. The Hungarian Guard was formed in 2007 following violent anti-government demonstrations in 2006. It was formed with their stated intent, to preserve the country's traditions and culture. The Hungarian Guard staged marches in various towns wearing uniforms depicting "Gypsy crime" as a threat to security. The court ruled that the Hungarian Guard curtailed the freedom and the rights of others and "triggered a risk of violence" during its rallies.34
[53] Prior to the Supreme Court ruling, the Guard continued to take in new members despite being banned. During one ceremony on private property, police arrived and initiated legal proceedings against 176 people for participating in a banned organization. In November before the Supreme Court decision, in Sajobabony, local Roma attempted to break up a Hungarian Guard assembly during a Jobbik party political rally. After a group of Roma assaulted one of the organizers, Jobbik and the Magyar Garda members proceeded to the Romani section of town. Several clashes and damage to vehicles resulted. Police responded by establishing several checkpoints leading into town.35
[54] I appreciate that there has been a rise in violent crime against the Roma particularly between when in June and August 2008, a series of physical attacks against Roma resulted in the killing of nine persons and injuries to many others. In response, the national police chief doubled the number of detectives in the special unit assigned to the case to 100. On August 21, 2008 police arrested four suspects and no additional attacks of this nature occurred after the arrests. When non-governmental groups criticized the investigation into the crimes, police ordered an internal disciplinary proceeding to identify the alleged mistakes, and these resulted in disciplinary measures against two police officers.36 I have considered a news article provided by the claimants,37 regarding the new Hungarian Laws.
[55] President Laslo Solyom stated publicly that Hungary must change treatment of Roma, when responding to attacks on Hungary's Roma, which make up 5-7 percent of the 10 million population. Analysts say the Roma have become a target for frustration over slowing economic growth, particularly with the global economic crisis.
[56] In addition to a police force and mechanisms to seek redress relating to complaints against the police, Hungary has other instruments to help Roma with problems they may encounter. The Ministry of Justice and Law Enforcement, which oversees the Hungarian National Police (HNP), operated the Roma Anti-Discrimination Legal Service network that provides free legal aid to Roma in cases where they encountered discrimination based on their ethnicity.38 The expansion of the Roma Anti-Discrimination Customer Service Network means that in 2008 there were 30 lawyers at 47 municipalities in the country and the coordination of the program in the Ministry is ensured by a desk officer for Roma affairs. Clients can obtain information on the customer service contact details of Network lawyers in 24 hours a day. There was a nationwide campaign promoting the network and toll-free number. Since 2004, the Network has handled more than 7,200 cases.39
[57] There is also recourse to the Equal Treatment Authority. Since 2005 it has provided individuals with a direct avenue of redress for violations of the prohibition of discrimination in a variety of public and private law relationships. In the first year, there were nearly 500 complaints lodged and the number has risen steadily every since. There are also other remedies such as seeking compensation through the courts, or turning to one of the Parliamentary Commissioners where public authorities are concerned.40 There is also the Roma Police Officers' Association in Hungary and Roma are able to file a complaint to the association. The complaints mostly concern discrimination in employment, discriminatory treatment, and discrimination by law enforcement authorities or police officers.41
[58] If the principal claimant faces discrimination, he has recourse to all these programs for assistance should he need it.
[59] The Hungarian government has taken a number of legal and institutional measures to improve the situation of the Romani minority. According to the open Society Institute, Hungary has one of the most advanced systems for minority protection in the region. The Hungarian Government lists a number of institutions within the state apparatus, including the Roma Integration Department within the Ministry of Social Affairs and Labor, the main government body responsible for Romani integration and the Council of Roma Integration, a consultative, advisory body, which coordinates measures between various ministries on the social inclusion of Roma.42 Hungary has taken a number of initiatives relating to the situation of the Roma including, education, employment, housing, health, and political representation.43
[60] Hungary faces criticisms44 regarding the implementation of the laws that it has enacted to address the discrimination and persecution of its minorities, especially the Romani. While there may be motivation within the central government to have its laws enforced, there is difficulty in implementing the enforcement of these laws at the local level, and resources routinely fail to reach the groups with the greatest needs. The criticisms against Hungary may be deserved, but what is important to note is that Hungary is a part of the European Union and is therefore responsible for upholding a number of various standards to maintain its membership in the Union. For instance, the European Commission against Racism and Intolerance (ECRI) was established by the Council of Europe. It is an independent human rights monitoring body specialized in questions relating to racism and intolerance. It is composed of independent and impartial members, who are appointed on the basis of their moral authority and recognized expertise in dealing with racism, xenophobia, anti-Semitism and intolerance. The ECRI published a report on Hungary in which gives praise to Hungary for its accomplishments, cites issues of concern, and gives recommendation for future action.45 What is important to note, in this instance, is that Hungary is not an island unto its own, but it is a responsible member of the European Union and reports regularly to the governance structures within that Union. Even if criticism of Hungary's measures to combat racism is warranted, particularly against the Romani population, on a balance of probabilities, Hungary is taking the measures to implement the standards that are mandated as a member of the European Union.
[61] The documentary evidence indicates that there is widespread discrimination against the Roma and evidence of specific incidents of persecution.46 These persecutory acts are often promoted and carried out by right-wing extremists groups, such as members of the Hungarian Guard, whose members have continued their activities under different names and in newly formed different organizations.47 Additionally, the right-wing Jobbik Party is an "extreme right-wing" political party with nationalistic roots and a strong anti-Roma agenda.
According to Amnesty International:48
There were nine attacks against Romani communities in 2008 and 2009 which bore similar characteristics. The perpetrators used Molotov cocktails and firearms, and the victims of the attacks lived in various places across the country. The attacks caused fear among the Romani community throughout the country. The police reacted by taking several measures within a programme to enhance community safety. In April 2009 they were extended to "vulnerable settlements" where police believed similar attacks could be expected. These areas were patrolled at night and in early morning house.
[62] Counsel submitted that state protection in Hungary is inadequate, and provided documentary evidence relating to his submissions.49 He also submits numerous reports to indicate that the rise of right-wing extremism incites violence against the Roma and the Roma face discrimination and persecution on many levels. There are similar incidents cited in the Board's own documents.
[63] Violent attacks against Roma continued, generating strong public concern and intense disputes as to the existence and scale of racially motivated crimes. Human rights NGOs complained that law enforcement authorities, prosecutors, and courts were reluctant to recognize racial motivation for many crimes.50
[64] According to multiple sources, the Hungarian Guard and other far-right organizations held demonstrations across the country with the goal of inciting "prejudice against the Roma". There were also reports of anti-Roma marches by members of the Jobbik party, as well as the Hungarian Guard, that were held in rural towns to "intimidate Roma.51
[65] The panel has also considered the Affidavit of XXXX XXXX - She is a citizen of the U.S., a human rights advocate, who has been following the situation of the Roma minority in Central Europe since 1994. She resided in the Czech Republic from 1994 until the start of 2010. All of her examples are no later than 2010 and much of the material is dated prior to 2010. However, much of what is contained is also in the Board's documentation. This document does not refer to the specific claim nor does it address any of the efforts being made by the current Hungarian government.52
[66] With respect to XXXX, whose livelihood is generated from his views on the situation, I find that, overall, the evidence from the many independent sources before the panel who have no vested interest in their assessment of the situation in Hungary for minorities, indicates that there has been success associated with every program implemented. There is empirical evidence of the treatment of racist violence under the criminal system, with successful investigation and prosecution of violent attacks as set out above.53
[67] I find that the government of Hungary is taking important steps to ensure state protection is available to their citizens including those of Roma ethnicity and that the claimants did not take reasonable steps to avail themselves of that protection. I acknowledge counsel's submission as supported by the country documents that the protection is not perfect and there are many areas that require improvement including in regard to the corruption of some police. However, I still find that, in this case, state protection would be available and, although in need of improvement, is adequate.
[68] Just because the police did not apprehend the culprits, or that the claimants' complaint was not pursued with the diligence that the claimants would have preferred does not mean that state protection in their home country is not adequate. There may be many factors that could contribute to this, including lack of physical evidence, lack of suspects higher priorities for the police, and lack of witnesses. The Federal Court has stated that the Court should not impose on other states a standard of "effective" protection that police forces in our own country, regrettably, sometimes only aspire to. It is open to the panel to determine if the state was unable to protect them, not in the absolute sense, but rather to a degree that was reasonable having regard to the circumstances of the claimants.54
[69] There is no persuasive evidence before me that the principal claimant would face persecution or, on a balance of probabilities, face a risk to his life or to cruel and unusual treatment or punishment or a danger of torture, if he returned to Hungary. As stated in the foregoing, his wife's ethnicity as a Hungarian and her allegations as contained in the PIF are not supported in the objective evidence. Since her oral testimony with respect to her husband's allegations is essentially the same, on this basis, her claim must fail. As stated earlier, there was insufficient persuasive evidence with respect to the principal claimant's mother. With respect to the children, the fears were speculative in the future, with the exception of the son XXXX XXXX. However, in the absence of medical psychological evidence to corroborate these allegations, his claim must also fail.
[70]
For all these reasons, I find that the claimants are not Convention refugees or persons in need of protection, whether under section 96 or section 97 of the Act, as state protection is available to them.
(signed)
'Roslyn Ahara'
Roslyn Ahara
March 19, 2013
